result:
I’ve been thinking about the moment when a piece of music first hijacks your bloodstream. Not the polite appreciation you summon in a concert hall, back straight, program folded like a paper cathedral in your lap, but the sudden, almost violent possession that happens when a certain major seventh or a frayed cough of distortion slips past your defenses and begins rearranging your personal geology. The first time it happened to me was in a supermarket cereal aisle— fluorescent noon, linoleum echoing with wheel-squeal, the air thick with the synthetic honesty of fake vanilla. Overhead, a song I had heard a dozen times on adult-contemporary radio revealed a hidden fugue line in the backing vocals, a ghost countermelody that felt like discovering an extra room in a house I’d lived in for years. My peripheral vision collapsed; the cartoon tiger on the cornflakes box stared at me like he knew. I stood there, slack-jawed, holding a box I didn’t even like, while something inside my rib cage performed unauthorized renovations. I must have looked like I’d just remembered my own death. A kid nearby asked his mom if the man was okay. She pulled him closer, whispered something about “not staring at strange.” But strangeness was the whole point. Music does that—turns you into a stranger to yourself, then hands you a better passport.

Years later, driving across Nevada at an hour when even the moon seems to doze, I understood that the opposite can happen too: you hijack the music instead. It was 3:00 a.m., I was the only car on a road so straight it felt like a dare, and the playlist I’d dubbed “Desert Anesthesia” was failing to keep me awake. My brain had begun that slow dissolve where the white lines in the periphery start to look like Morse code. Then a track came on that I’d uploaded out of loyalty to a friend’s synth-noir side project—tinny drums, narcoleptic bass, a chord progression that refused resolution. Halfway through, my exhaustion began to conduct it. I don’t mean metaphorically. My foot on the cruise-control accelerator pulsed micro-faster every time the kick drum hit; my breathing synced to the filter sweep. I was remastering the song in real time, turning the wheel slightly to pan the stereo field. The Mojave became the reverb chamber. By the bridge, I swear I heard harmonies that weren’t on the recording—phantom voices blooming in the long black between Joshua trees. I was simultaneously driver, producer, and audience. When the song ended, I pulled over, engine idling like a stunned metronome, and cried for no reason I could name. It wasn’t joy; it wasn’t fear. Just the raw acknowledgment that something—call it mind, call it electricity—had stepped out of its usual costume and tried on someone else’s skin for size.

I keep a small notebook now titled “Thresholds.” Not for melodies I might later steal, but for moments when perception slips its leash. The smell of library dust that suddenly smells like my grandmother’s closet in 1987. The afternoon light on a bank façade that for three seconds makes every pedestrian look typoed into the scene, too saturated, as if the universe is mocking my monitor settings. The time a stranger’s sneeze in the metro harmonized with the train’s brakes and a baby’s laugh, producing a major-seventh chord so immaculate I looked around for hidden cameras. These are warnings or invitations—sometimes both—that the world is perforated. Most days I treat reality like it’s the only item on the menu. Then a perforation tears open and I remember we’re dining ala carte across multiple dimensions, we just keep forgetting to ask for the specials.

There’s a scientific term I love: “umwelt.” Every creature swims inside its own sensory bubble, an ecosystem of smells, textures, magnetic fields, or color bands unavailable to the customer at the next table. Humans, stuck between the pride of consciousness and the embarrassment of limitation, pretend one umwelt fits all. Art is the revenge of the repressed senses. A painting teaches you to see ultramarine the way honeybees see ultraviolet. A novel installs a new emotional plug-in, and suddenly your own memories run on cleaner voltage. And music—music is the cheat code to rewire the operating system entirely, no firmware update required.

Sometimes I fantasize about a Ministry of Umwelt Expansion, a clandestine agency issuing passports to parallel perceptions. Agents would drop random “invitations” in public space: a sidewalk tile that squeaks a D-flat when stepped on; a library book hollowed out to contain a mirror angled so you glimpse your own eye in mid-blink; a vending machine that dispenses only one flavor of soda—”Saudade”—label written in disappearing ink. Afterward, you’d walk around unable to articulate what exactly has changed, but grocery-store muzak would now arrive in antique Latin, traffic lights would taste faintly of cinnamon, your dreams would feature background characters humming the same four notes you heard in the subway three weeks earlier. You’d become a tourist in your own life, which is the only way any of us ever leave home without dying.

The trouble, of course, is that tourism depends on return tickets. After my Nevada possession, I spent weeks chasing that same merger of body and song. I sat in parked cars looping the track, drove pointless orbits around the city at 3:00 a.m., messed with EQ settings until the waveform looked like seismograph output from a tectonic sigh. Nothing. It was only when I gave up—when I let music become background again—that the next visitation happened: in a dentist’s chair, root canal in progress, drill whining a perfect fifth above the office radio. Under nitrous, the whine modulated, phase-shifted, locked into a groove with the radio’s soft-rock cliche, and suddenly I was floating above my own open mouth, watching a tiny orchestra of hygienists play my nerves like Stradivarius strings. The dentist later said I kept humming “a sort of reggae beat” although my face was numb. I left with a prescription and a new entry in the notebook: “Pain at 3300 Hz = doorbell to the soul.”

I used to think enlightenment would feel like clarity, a final answer. Now I suspect it’s more like fracture: cracks that let other frequencies leak in. Enlightenment, or maybe just maturity—same thing, different disco ball. The adolescent self believes coherence equals identity: I like these bands, vote this way, fear these outcomes, therefore I exist. The porous self—the one that’s toured a few neighboring umwelts—knows coherence is just a local ordinance, often enforced by trauma or marketing. Step outside, and ordinances change. You can hum your way back, but the tune carries saltwater, sagebrush, dentist-drill, supermarket vanilla. People notice you walk differently, as if your bones now serve multiple unions. Sometimes you leak music when you meant to speak.

This leaking frightens those who police the borders of normal. They have clipboards, checkboxes: Does he always respond to questions with a melodic interval? Has she been seen tasting colors? I understand the fear. Once you permit one perforation, who knows how many others will follow? Before long, the sky might declare independence, start holding elections you didn’t even know you cared about. But here’s the secret: the sky is already campaigning. It drops pamphlets in the form of sunsets, holds rallies at dawn, funds punk-rock drum lines inside summer storms. Pretending otherwise doesn’t preserve coherence; it just turns life into a very long waiting room where the magazines are all out of date.

So I keep the notebook, hum off-key, brake for imaginary cadences. I don’t crave constant transcendence—that way lies the guru industrial complex and premium-priced retreats promising “transformation in a weekend, lunch included.” I want something humbler: the reflex to notice when a new frequency punches a hole in the wall, and the courage to crawl through even if the other side smells like nothing I’ve named. I want to remain the kid who once stared at cereal-box tigers like they might answer back, but also the adult who knows the answer arrives in a chord you’ve never heard before and will never hear the same way again.

Word count told me to stop at a thousand, but the metronome in my chest is asynchronous; it keeps adding eighth-note rests. Maybe that’s the final entry for tonight: “Off the page, the song continues—learn to breathe in 7/4.”

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
